data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-05T01:36:20.475Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were always implemented as sync-over-async internally. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 / EF Core 11, this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely for the Cosmos DB provider.


Let me clarify the timeline here. EF Core 9 started throwing by default for synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider, but you could opt in to sync-over-async behavior through configuration. When moving to .NET 11, that opt-in mechanism is completely removed, so any synchronous LINQ methods like `ToList()` or `First()` will no longer work.

I should note that .NET 11 hasn't been released yet, so I'm reasoning based on the clear trajectory EF Core has been taking toward eliminating sync-over-async support entirely for Cosmos DB. The pattern suggests the opt-in that worked in .NET 10 will be gone, forcing a migration to async-only code.
